title: On some questions about minimal log discrepancies
speaker: Mircea Mustata
abstract: The minimal log discrepancy is a measure of singularities of pairs. While akin to the log canonical threshold, it turns out to be much more difficult to study, with many questions still open. I will discuss a question about the boundedness of divisors that compute the minimal log discrepancy on a fixed germ. This is joint work (in progress) with Yusuke Nakamura.

proof_stages.label: Valuative definitions of LCTs and MLDs
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up smooth ambient varieties, R-ideals, divisorial valuations, centers, orders of vanishing, log discrepancies, and the definitions of log canonical thresholds and minimal log discrepancies, emphasizing their computation on resolutions.

proof_stages.label: Open MLD conjectures and ACC context
proof_stages.summary: Ambro semicontinuity and Shokurov's ACC conjecture for MLDs are presented as the main open problems, with their relevance to termination of flips and with known results contrasted against the better-understood LCT case.

proof_stages.label: Bounded divisor question and main results
proof_stages.summary: For fixed dimension and finite coefficient set, the speaker formulates the question of whether an MLD can always be computed by a divisor of bounded log discrepancy, states a weaker theorem bounding only the order along the maximal ideal, and records positive answers in the surface and toric settings.

proof_stages.label: Surface reduction to controlled blow-up recurrences
proof_stages.summary: Assuming the maximal-ideal order bound, the surface case is reduced to a sequence of point blow-ups with only boundedly many non-free steps, and recurrence formulas are derived for alpha_i, the MLD contribution, and beta_i, the residual order at the center.

proof_stages.label: Surface contradiction from the three beta regimes
proof_stages.summary: The blow-up recurrences are used to show successively that beta_i>1 occurs only boundedly often, beta_i=1 cannot persist past one step, and beta_i<1 also gives only bounded length because alpha_i would otherwise exceed the surface MLD bound.

proof_stages.label: Generic-limit proof sketch for the maximal-ideal order bound
proof_stages.summary: Theorem A is sketched by passing a sequence of ideals to a generic limit, using LCT behavior to split into a case with an m-adic margin that directly bounds orders and a boundary case where a divisor computing LCT=1 transfers back to compute MLD=0 for infinitely many approximants.

examples.note: Taking all exponents in the R-ideal equal to a single rational number is mentioned only to make the general R-ideal notation feel concrete.

examples.name: Principal surface ideal with monomial tangent cone
examples.note: The simplified case a=(f)^q with qd=1 and tangent cone x^d illustrates why two consecutive multiplicity-one stages force the first exceptional divisor to compute the MLD.
examples.engagement_reason: He specializes to a principal surface ideal f^q, derives qd=1, identifies the tangent cone as x^d, and uses blowup and semicontinuity computations to show the first exceptional divisor computes the MLD.

passes.reason: The tangent cone being a pure power of one line is a collapsed non-reduced plane-curve tangent cone used in a proof case, not as a counterexample.

examples.name: Sequence of hypersurfaces forming a generic limit
examples.note: A sequence of hypersurfaces assembled into a nonstandard polynomial and then a power series is used as an informal illustration of the generic-limit construction.
examples.engagement_reason: He describes running the generic-limit construction on a sequence of hypersurfaces/ideals and then analyzes the resulting limit via log canonical thresholds in two cases.
